---
title: Container data types
format: 
    pdf: default
    ipynb: default
jupyter: python3
---

## Overview

## list {#sec-list}

list[bool] any and all


## set {#sec-set}

## dict {#sec-dict}

## tuple {#sec-tuple}

## iterating through list/set/dict/tuple {#sec-iterating}

## list/set/dict/tuple comprehensions {#sec-iterating}

expression for variable in iterable if condition
[x*x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]

## list/set/dict as mutable datatypes {#sec-mutable}




list set dict tuple

### Mathematical angle
This is a good moment to compare:
- lists with finite sequences,
- tuples with fixed records,
- dictionaries with finite maps.

for strings: 
x[0] x[1:4] 
immutable 
x[0] = "Z"


Lists - These are collections of values in the CSV format. Lists are mutable objects.
Tuples - These are like lists, but immutable.
Dictionaries - These are collections of key-value pairs defined using curly braces.
Sets - They can’t have duplicates, and are mutable.


```{python3}
mi, ma = a if a < b else b, b if a < b else a
print(f"Mminimum and maximum are {mi} and {ma}.")
```


Exercise

For two strings `s` and `t`, write a function `myCount(s, t)`, which counts the numbers of occurrences of `t` in `s`. Unlike `count`, it should count the number of positions in `s` starting with `t`. For example, `"bababab".count("bab")` gives 2, but `"bababab".myCount("bab")` should give 3.
